title: Karnıyarık
subtitle: Oven-braised aubergines stuffed with spiced lamb and tomato
excerpt: Whole aubergines split and filled with a spiced lamb and tomato mixture, then baked until the aubergine collapses around the filling and the top is capped with a sliver of tomato and green pepper. A classic of Ottoman home cooking, slow, soft, deeply savoury.
sourceNotes: Karnıyarık (the name means split belly) is documented in Ottoman and Turkish culinary literature from the nineteenth century. It appears in early twentieth-century Turkish household cookbooks as a standard preparation for the meat and aubergine combinations that characterise much of Anatolian cooking. The dish is related to the aubergine and meat preparations of the broader Ottoman kitchen, which include imam bayıldı (meatless) and patlıcan kebabı (grilled). Aubergine has been central to Anatolian cooking since its introduction via Persian and Arabian trade routes in the medieval period.

content.text: Karnıyarık is the more substantial cousin of imam bayıldı. Where imam bayıldı is meatless (aubergine softened in olive oil with onion, tomato, and garlic), karnıyarık adds spiced lamb mince, which makes it a proper main course. Both rely on the same technique: the aubergine must absorb oil generously before filling, otherwise it stays dense and bitter rather than collapsing into softness around the stuffing.

The green pepper and tomato placed on top before baking are not optional garnish. As they cook, they release liquid that bastes the filling and keeps the top from drying out. The finished dish should be served with plain rice and yoghurt alongside. It reheats well.

items.name: Green pepper
items.prepNote: 1 diced for filling, 1 sliced lengthways for topping

items.name: Tomato
items.prepNote: 1 diced for filling, 1 sliced for topping

items.name: Flat-leaf parsley
items.prepNote: finely chopped, plus more to finish

items.name: Olive oil
items.prepNote: 3 for frying the aubergines, 3 for the filling and baking

content.text: Preheat the oven to 190°C (fan 170°C). Score each {{aubergine}} along one side with a deep slit, leaving 2 cm at each end so the aubergine stays intact. Salt generously inside the slit and let stand for 15 minutes. Pat dry. Warm 3 tablespoons of the {{olive-oil}} in a wide frying pan over medium-high heat. Fry the aubergines, turning occasionally, for 8 to 10 minutes until well coloured on all sides and beginning to soften. Transfer to a roasting tin, slit-side up.

In the same pan over medium heat, add the remaining olive oil. Cook the {{onion}} onion and diced green pepper for 5 minutes until soft. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic, {{cumin-ground}} of cumin, and {{paprika-sweet}} of paprika and cook for 1 minute. Add the {{lamb-mince}} and cook, breaking it up, until no pink remains, about 5 minutes. Add the diced {{tomato}} tomato and the {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes. Season well with salt and pepper. Simmer for 5 minutes until the filling is moist but not wet. Stir in the {{parsley-flat}} of parsley.

Use a spoon to gently open each aubergine slit and press the flesh inside aside to make room. Fill each aubergine generously with the lamb filling, mounding it slightly. Top each with a slice of tomato and a strip of green pepper. Add 100 ml of water to the roasting tin. Bake for 35 to 40 minutes until the aubergines are completely soft and collapsing around the filling and the tops are golden. Rest for 10 minutes before serving. Finish with parsley and serve with plain rice and yoghurt.

items.fix: Fry until clearly softened and collapsed before filling; bake until the flesh yields completely when pressed
items.cause: Not fried long enough before filling; aubergine must begin to soften before it goes in the oven
items.symptom: Aubergine is still firm and dense after 40 minutes of baking

items.fix: Add the full 100 ml of water to the tin; the tomato topping also bastes the filling as it cooks
items.cause: Too little tomato; or baked without water in the tin

items.fix: Salt inside the slit for 15 minutes before frying; aubergine absorbs a lot of oil and must be given the opportunity to do so
items.cause: Salting step skipped, or not enough oil during frying

items.fix: Taste the filling before stuffing and adjust salt, pepper, and cumin; it should be well seasoned before it goes into the oven

content.text: Karnıyarık is a home-kitchen dish, more than a restaurant one. Cooks make it for family lunches, with plain rice, yoghurt, and a salad on the side. Aubergine and spiced lamb is a classic Turkish pair, used in many dishes under many names. Karnıyarık is one of the best of them. The soft, oil-rich aubergine and the meaty, savoury filling go together well, and the dish tastes better after a few hours' rest.
